steps.heading: The puzzle
steps.body: You're working with an LLM that has a 4,000 token context window. Your conversation so far uses 3,200 tokens. You want to add a document for analysis. The document is 1,200 words long. Approximately how many tokens is the document, and will it fit?

steps.heading: The math
steps.body: Rule of thumb: 1 token ≈ 0.75 words, so 1,200 words ≈ 1,600 tokens. Adding 1,600 tokens to your 3,200 token conversation = 4,800 tokens total. The 4,000 token limit would be exceeded by 800 tokens. The document won't fit — you need to summarize it first or start a new conversation.

steps.question: A document is 750 words long. Approximately how many tokens is it?
options: About 250 tokens
About 500 tokens
About 1,000 tokens
Exactly 750 tokens

steps.question: Your context window is 8,000 tokens. You've used 7,500. A document is 600 words (~800 tokens). What should you do?
options: Send it — it will fit exactly
It won't fit; summarize the document or start a new conversation
Double the context window size
The model will automatically compress the conversation

steps.heading: The scenario
steps.body: You ask an LLM: 'What were the main findings of the Smith & Johnson (2023) study on LLM accuracy?' The model responds with a detailed, confident 3-paragraph summary with specific statistics and quotes. The problem: you made up this study. It doesn't exist.

steps.heading: Why this happens
steps.body: LLMs generate plausible-sounding text based on patterns, not verified facts. When asked about a non-existent study, the model fabricates details that sound academic — realistic journal names, plausible author names, believable statistics. It doesn't know it's wrong; it's doing exactly what it was trained to do: predict the most likely next token.

steps.question: An LLM confidently cites a study that doesn't exist. What should you do?
options: Trust it — the model wouldn't cite something fake
Verify the source independently before using or referencing it
Ask the model to double-check its answer
Switch to a different AI model

steps.question: Which technique best reduces hallucination risk for factual questions?
options: Using shorter prompts
Providing the source document in the prompt and asking the model to answer from it
Asking the model to be confident
Using a lower temperature setting

steps.heading: Diagnose the problem
steps.body: A developer sends this prompt: 'Explain async/await'. The model returns a 500-word explanation covering JavaScript history, promises, callback hell, and modern async patterns — far more than needed. The developer wanted a one-line explanation for a comment in their code. What went wrong?

steps.heading: The fix
steps.body: The prompt was missing: the audience (a one-line code comment), the length constraint (one sentence), and the context (going into code, not documentation). The improved prompt: 'Explain async/await in one sentence suitable for a code comment aimed at a junior developer.' This version has a clear goal, constraint, and audience.

steps.question: Which revised prompt would best produce a short, code-comment-friendly explanation?
options: Explain async/await simply
What is async/await? Be brief.
Explain async/await in exactly one sentence for a code comment read by junior developers
Tell me about async/await in JavaScript

steps.question: A prompt gets a response that is accurate but 10x longer than needed. The most likely cause is:
options: The model is broken
The prompt lacked a length constraint
The topic is too complex for the model
The API has a minimum response length

steps.heading: The scenario
steps.body: You're building a tool that extracts bug information from user reports. You send this prompt: 'Extract the bug details from this report: [report text]'. The model returns a readable paragraph. Your code tries to parse it as JSON and crashes. How do you fix the prompt?

steps.heading: The fix
steps.body: Specify the exact JSON schema in the prompt: 'Extract bug details from this report and return ONLY valid JSON with this structure: { "title": string, "severity": "low|medium|high", "steps_to_reproduce": string[], "expected": string, "actual": string }. Return only the JSON with no surrounding text.' The model will follow the schema reliably.

steps.question: What should you add to a prompt to ensure the model returns parseable JSON?
options: Ask the model to be precise
Specify the exact JSON schema and instruct it to return ONLY JSON
Tell the model you are a developer
Use a shorter prompt

steps.question: Your code parses LLM output as JSON but sometimes fails. The most likely cause is:
options: The model is down
The model occasionally adds text outside the JSON despite instructions
JSON parsing is buggy in your language
The model uses a non-standard JSON format

steps.heading: The scenario
steps.body: You're automating a daily report: fetch data from a database, filter for the past 24 hours, generate a summary, send it to Slack. Simple — until you map it fully. The database sometimes returns null for missing values. The Slack webhook occasionally fails. The summary format changes monthly. Which parts are automation risks?

steps.heading: The analysis
steps.body: Three risks emerge: (1) Null values — the filter step needs null handling. (2) Slack failures — the send step needs retry logic and failure alerting. (3) Format changes — the summary step is fragile if hardcoded. Good automation handles each: validate nulls, retry Slack with exponential backoff, and make the format configurable.

steps.question: An automation step that calls an external API has no error handling. What is the risk?
options: The automation will run slower
A transient API failure will silently break the automation with no alert
The API will block your requests
The step will be skipped automatically

steps.question: Which part of an automation should be made configurable rather than hardcoded?
options: The language the automation is written in
The number of steps
Output formats that change over time
The automation name

steps.heading: The scenario
steps.body: A test was written 6 months ago and now fails every deployment. The locator: page.locator('.btn-primary.submit-action:nth-child(3)'). The developer redesigned the UI — the button is still there, still visible, still labeled 'Submit', but in a different position and with different CSS classes. How do you fix the locator?

steps.heading: The fix
steps.body: Replace the fragile CSS selector with a role-based locator: page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' }). This locator doesn't care about CSS classes or position — it finds the button by what it does (button role) and what it says (Submit). If the button's label never changes, this locator will survive any UI redesign.

steps.question: A test breaks every time the UI is restyled. The most likely cause is:
options: The test logic is wrong
Locators depend on CSS classes or element position
The browser has changed
The test runs too fast

steps.question: Which locator is most resilient to UI changes for a 'Confirm' button?
options: page.locator('#btn-23')
page.locator('button.confirm-btn')
page.locator('div > button:first-child')
page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Confirm' })

steps.heading: Trace the execution
Following the data

steps.question: What value does `result` hold after `function multiply(a, b) { return a * b; } let result = multiply(4, 5);` runs?
options: undefined

steps.question: What does `x` equal after `function addOne(n) { return n + 1; } let x = addOne(addOne(3));`?
options: "addOne(addOne(3))"

steps.heading: Tracing a for loop
Tracing a while loop

steps.question: After `let total = 0; for (let i = 1; i <= 4; i++) { total = total + i; }` runs, what does `total` equal?
After the while-loop trace above runs, what value does `steps` end up holding?

steps.heading: Spotting what belongs in a page object
From raw steps to a method

steps.question: If the search input's selector changes from `#search-input` to `#searchBox`, how many places need updating when using the `SearchPage` class above?
options: Five — once per test file
One — inside the SearchPage class
Zero — Playwright updates it automatically
Every test and the page object

steps.question: What does calling `searchPage.search('laptop')` do, based on the class definition above?
options: Opens a new browser tab
Fills the search input with 'laptop' and clicks the search button
Returns the number of results as text
Logs in the user

steps.heading: Spotting a parallel-unsafe test
Making it isolation-safe

steps.question: What's the risk of the first version of the test running in multiple parallel workers?
options: It will run twice as fast
All workers may try to create a project with the same name and collide
Playwright will refuse to run it
It will use less memory

steps.question: How does adding `Date.now()` to the project name fix the parallel execution problem?
options: It makes the test run faster
It gives each parallel run a unique name, preventing collisions
It disables parallel execution for this test
It skips the assertion step

steps.heading: Choosing the right verb
steps.body: Imagine you're building a to-do list app that talks to an API. Adding a new task means sending its details to the server so it can be stored — that's creating a brand-new resource, which calls for `POST /tasks`.

steps.heading: Reading, changing, and removing
steps.body: Once tasks exist, viewing them is `GET /tasks` (or `GET /tasks/5` for one specific task), marking one complete without touching its other fields is `PATCH /tasks/5`, and removing it entirely is `DELETE /tasks/5`. Each action maps to exactly one HTTP method because each expresses a different intent.

steps.question: Which request would you send to add a brand-new task to the list?
options: GET /tasks
POST /tasks
DELETE /tasks
PUT /tasks/5

steps.question: Which request marks task 5 as complete without changing its title or due date?
options: PATCH /tasks/5
GET /tasks/5
POST /tasks/5
DELETE /tasks/5

steps.heading: Spotting the missing piece
The corrected request

steps.question: What was wrong with the original `body: { text: 'Great post!' }` line?
options: Nothing — it would work fine
fetch requires the body to be a string, but a raw object was passed
The text was too long
POST requests cannot have a body

steps.question: Why does the corrected version add a `Content-Type: application/json` header?
options: To make the request run faster
To tell the server how to interpret the body it receives
To authenticate the user
To prevent the request from being cached

steps.heading: Matching the tool to the task
steps.body: An agent's goal is "find out if it will rain tomorrow in Lisbon and email me a summary." Looking at its available tools — `getWeather(city, date)`, `sendEmail(to, subject, body)`, and `searchWeb(query)` — the first step is clearly `getWeather("Lisbon", "tomorrow")`: it directly answers the question being asked, with no guesswork needed.

steps.heading: Chaining tools toward the goal
steps.body: Once the weather result comes back — say `{ condition: "rain", chance: 80 }` — the agent has what it needs to write a summary. The final step is `sendEmail(to, "Weather Summary", "80% chance of rain in Lisbon tomorrow.")`. Notice how each tool call depends on the result of the one before it — the agent couldn't write the email before knowing the forecast.

steps.question: Given the goal 'find out if it will rain tomorrow in Lisbon and email me a summary,' which tool should the agent call first?
options: sendEmail, to notify the user it's starting
getWeather, to find out the forecast before writing anything
searchWeb, to look up what Lisbon is
None — the agent should guess the weather

steps.question: Why can't the agent call `sendEmail` before calling `getWeather` in this scenario?
options: sendEmail only works in the morning
The email's content depends on the forecast result, which doesn't exist yet
Tools must always be called in alphabetical order
Email tools require a weather tool to be installed first

steps.heading: Decomposing a goal
steps.body: Goal: "Find a recipe that uses chicken and rice, and create a shopping list for it." Broken into steps: (1) search for a chicken-and-rice recipe, (2) read the ingredient list from the result, (3) compare it against what's already on hand, and (4) produce a shopping list of what's missing. Each step produces information the next step depends on.

steps.heading: When a step doesn't go as planned
steps.body: Suppose step 1 returns no usable recipes. Blindly continuing to step 2 would mean working from nothing. A resilient agent instead recognizes the dead end and adapts — searching with different terms ("chicken rice dinner easy"), trying a different source, or reporting back that it couldn't find a suitable recipe rather than inventing one.

steps.question: In the recipe example, why does step 2 (reading the ingredient list) depend on step 1 (finding a recipe)?
options: It doesn't — the steps could run in any order
Step 2 needs the recipe that step 1 finds before it has any ingredients to read
Step 1 is optional
Steps 1 and 2 use the same tool

steps.question: If the recipe search in step 1 returns no results, what's the best response for the agent?
options: Continue to step 2 anyway and invent ingredients
Recognize the dead end and adapt — try a different search or report the issue
Shut down completely
Repeat the exact same search forever

steps.heading: Tracing a request through the system
steps.body: A user asks an assistant, "What's on my calendar tomorrow?" The host passes this to its AI model, which decides a calendar tool is needed. The client sends a request to the connected calendar server: "call `getEvents` for tomorrow's date." The server looks up the events and sends a response back with the results.

steps.heading: From response to answer
steps.body: The client hands that response to the host, which feeds it back to the AI model. The model turns the raw event data into a friendly summary — "You have a 10am meeting and a 2pm dentist appointment" — and the host displays it to the user. Each piece played a narrow, well-defined role in getting from question to answer.

steps.question: In the calendar example, which piece actually looks up the events and sends back the result?
options: The host
The AI model
The MCP server
The user

steps.question: After the server responds with the raw event data, what turns it into a friendly summary for the user?
options: The MCP client, by formatting the JSON
The AI model, by reasoning over the data and generating a response
The server, by sending a second message
The protocol handshake

steps.heading: Spotting an overloaded model
steps.body: A host connects to five MCP servers, each exposing 8-10 tools — nearly 50 tools in total. When the AI model is handed all 50 tool descriptions for a simple question like "what's the weather today," it has to wade through dozens of irrelevant options before finding the one it actually needs.

steps.heading: Filtering down to what's relevant
steps.body: A smarter approach narrows the list before it reaches the model — perhaps based on the kind of question being asked, recently used tools, or explicit categories. For "what's the weather today," surfacing just the weather-related tool, and a couple of close neighbors, lets the model decide quickly and correctly instead of getting lost in the noise.

steps.question: What problem does handing the AI model all 50 tool descriptions for a simple weather question create?
options: The model will run out of memory permanently
The model has to sift through many irrelevant options, making a good choice harder
MCP does not allow more than 10 tools
The servers will disconnect automatically

steps.question: What is one way to help the model choose well when many tools are available?
options: Always show every tool from every server, every time
Filter the list down to what's likely relevant to the current question
Disable all tools except one permanently
Remove tool descriptions entirely

steps.heading: Tracing a request downward
steps.body: A user submits a form to update their profile name. The presentation layer receives the new name and hands it to the business logic layer, which checks that it's valid — not empty, not too long — then asks the data layer to save it to the database. Each layer does its own job and trusts the layer below to do its part correctly.

steps.heading: Spotting a layering violation
steps.body: Now imagine the presentation layer directly opens a database connection to save the name, skipping the business logic layer entirely. The validation rules never run, so an empty or invalid name could be saved straight to the database. This is a classic layering violation — a shortcut that breaks the very separation the architecture was meant to provide.

steps.question: In the profile update example, which layer is responsible for checking that the new name is valid before it's saved?
options: The presentation layer
The business logic layer
The data layer
No layer — validation isn't needed

steps.question: What goes wrong if the presentation layer saves data directly to the database, skipping the business logic layer?
options: Nothing — it's a faster, equally safe shortcut
Validation rules never run, so invalid data could reach the database unchecked
The database will reject the connection automatically
The data layer will fix the problem on its own

steps.heading: Spotting the bottleneck
steps.body: A photo-sharing app is slow during peak hours. Investigation shows the web servers are mostly idle, but every request waits on the same single database to respond — it's overwhelmed by the volume of reads and writes. Adding more web servers wouldn't help; the database is the part actually limiting how much the system can handle.

steps.heading: Choosing a fix that targets the real problem
steps.body: Possible fixes that target the database bottleneck include adding a cache in front of it for frequently-read data, splitting reads and writes across different database instances, or optimizing the slowest queries. Each of these directly relieves pressure on the actual limiting factor — unlike adding more web servers, which would leave the bottleneck untouched.

steps.question: In the photo-sharing app example, why wouldn't adding more web servers fix the slowdown?
options: Web servers cannot be added to a running system
The actual bottleneck is the database, not the web servers, so adding more web servers wouldn't relieve the real pressure
More web servers always make a system slower
The app does not use a database

steps.question: Which of these would most directly help relieve pressure on an overwhelmed database?
options: Adding more web servers that all query it the same way
Adding a cache for frequently-read data so fewer requests reach the database directly
Changing the color scheme of the app
Renaming the database's tables

steps.heading: Spotting a vague content request
steps.body: Someone asks an AI tool: "write something for my business." The result comes back generic and unusable — it doesn't know what the business does, who reads it, or what it's for. The request was missing the very details that would have shaped a useful answer.

steps.heading: Rebuilding the request with the missing pieces
steps.body: A stronger version: "Write a 100-word Instagram caption announcing that my bakery is now open on Sundays, in a warm and friendly tone, aimed at local regulars." Now the AI knows the topic, the format, the length, the tone, and the audience — everything it needs to produce something genuinely close to useful on the first try.

steps.question: What was the main problem with the original request, 'write something for my business'?
options: It was too long
It lacked the context — topic, audience, tone, format — needed to produce something useful
AI tools cannot write about businesses
It used the word 'business' incorrectly

steps.question: What makes the rewritten request more likely to produce a useful result?
options: It is written in all capital letters
It specifies the topic, format, length, tone, and audience clearly
It avoids mentioning the business at all
It is shorter than the original

steps.heading: Choosing a good fit for AI assistance
steps.body: A team lead has two tasks: (1) drafting a friendly internal reminder about an upcoming team lunch, and (2) deciding whether to lay off part of the team due to a budget shortfall. The first is a low-stakes, repetitive communication task — a strong candidate for AI assistance with a quick human review. The second involves people's livelihoods, sensitive judgment, and major consequences — something that calls for careful human deliberation, not a quick generated draft.

steps.heading: Recognizing what changes the calculus
steps.body: What separates these isn't the topic itself but the stakes and the kind of judgment required: routine, low-risk, easily-reviewed tasks are a good fit for AI assistance, while high-stakes decisions involving real consequences for people call for careful human judgment, with AI playing — at most — a supporting role like organizing information.

steps.question: Which of the team lead's two tasks is a stronger candidate for AI-assisted drafting?
options: The layoff decision, because it's important
The lunch reminder, because it's routine, low-stakes, and easy to review
Neither — AI should never be used at work
Both equally — stakes don't matter

steps.question: What mainly determines whether a work task is a good fit for AI assistance?
options: How long the task takes to complete
The stakes involved and the kind of judgment the task requires
Whether the task involves any text at all
The time of day the task is performed

steps.heading: A request that goes nowhere
steps.body: Someone asks an AI: "fix my code." They paste in 200 lines with no explanation of what's wrong, what they expected, or what they're working on. The response comes back with generic suggestions that miss the actual problem entirely — the AI had no way to know what 'fixed' would even look like.

steps.heading: The same request, reframed
steps.body: A stronger version: "This function should return the highest score in the list, but it's returning undefined when the list is empty. Here's the function and an example input. What's causing this, and how would you fix it while keeping the same style as the rest of the file?" Now the AI has the goal, the symptom, the context, and a constraint — everything it needs to actually help.

steps.question: What was the core problem with the original request, 'fix my code'?
options: It included too much code
It lacked a clear goal, the specific symptom, and context the AI needed to help meaningfully
It was phrased too politely
AI tools cannot read code under any circumstances

steps.question: What does the rewritten version add that made it far more likely to produce a useful answer?
options: Nothing — both versions are equally vague
A clear description of expected vs. actual behavior, relevant context, and a constraint to respect
A demand that the AI respond instantly
A much longer block of unrelated code

steps.heading: A suite that nobody trusts anymore
steps.body: A team's test suite passes about 80% of the time, fails for different reasons each run, and takes 40 minutes to finish. Engineers have started merging code without waiting for it, because "it's probably just flaky." The suite still runs — but it has stopped doing its actual job.

steps.heading: Diagnosing what really broke
steps.body: The problem isn't that the suite is slow or imperfect — it's that it became untrustworthy. Shared state between tests, fragile selectors, and no clear failure messages turned a useful safety net into background noise. Rebuilding trust means isolating tests, stabilizing selectors, parallelizing safely, and making failures easy to diagnose — the exact practices from Stages 1 through 4 of your Playwright journey.

steps.question: What is the real underlying problem with the team's test suite?
options: It runs too quickly for anyone to notice results
It has become untrustworthy, so people have stopped relying on it
It contains too few tests to be useful
It only runs on one specific operating system

steps.question: Which combination of practices would most directly help rebuild trust in this suite?
options: Deleting all existing tests and starting over with no plan
Isolating tests, stabilizing selectors, running safely in parallel, and making failures easy to diagnose
Running the suite even less often so failures are seen less
Removing all assertions so tests can never fail
